pattern: DP over (step, position): at each step each position distributes its count to the three neighbors (left, stay, right), clamped to the array boundary; the effective array width is capped at min(arrLen, steps//2 + 1) because the pointer cannot reach further than steps//2 in one direction and still return.
hints: After all steps the pointer must sit at position 0; moving right k times requires at least k moves back left, so the pointer can never usefully exceed position steps//2 — cap the working range there.
Model it as dp[pos] = number of ways to be at position pos after the current step; initialise dp[0] = 1 and simulate each step by spreading each cell's count left, right, and in-place.
Accumulate everything modulo 10^9 + 7 and remember that left-of-0 and right-of-(arrLen-1) are walls — contributions that would leave the array are simply discarded.

edge_inputs.description: one step, array of length 1 — can only stay, one way
one step, array of length 2 — stay or move right; only staying returns to 0, one way
two steps, large array — can go right-left, left-right (invalid since index goes negative), or stay-stay
maximum steps with array length 1 — only path is stay every step, answer is 1
even steps with large arrLen — effective width is steps//2+1, not arrLen
odd number of steps, so pointer must make an odd number of direction changes — some naive counts include parity-illegal paths
arrLen exactly equals steps//2 + 1 — boundary is snug
arrLen=2 forces the pointer to stay near 0, drastically limiting valid paths

stress.description: maximum steps and large arrLen — tests modular arithmetic and the width cap
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    steps = max(1, min(size, 500))
    arrLen = rng.randint(steps // 2 + 1, 1000000)
    return [steps, arrLen]
stress.note: arrLen always larger than steps//2 so the cap kicks in; verifies large-mod result

stress.description: tight arrLen equal to 1 — only the all-stay path is valid
stress.note: answer is always 1; sanity check for DP base case
